core_intuitions: RLHF is a distributed-systems problem: the policy, critic, reference, reward model, and rollout engine are five distinct roles that must share a GPU cluster, and Ray actors map one-to-one onto those roles (each actor owns its own GPUs and communicates asynchronously through the object store).

The rollout engine holds a SECOND, read-only copy of the policy weights for generation; after every PPO update the trainer must PUSH the freshly-updated weights into that copy, and this weight-sync step is the defining engineering cost of the loop.

Weight-sync cost is bandwidth-bound: moving ~140 GB of bf16 params over NVLink vs InfiniBand vs PCIe differs by an order of magnitude, so sync latency as a FRACTION of the update step is what determines whether you sync every step or every N steps.

The central design axis is dynamic task graph (Ray: heterogeneous per-role GPU counts, Python controller, serialize across actor boundaries) vs static SPMD (Megatron: one uniform pre-wired NCCL topology for all roles, no Python in the hot path) — flexibility traded against communication efficiency.
Megatron reaches scale by combining three orthogonal parallelism axes (TP within a layer, PP across layers, DP across replicas) into one fixed 3D topology that every role must conform to.

The PPO iteration is a fan-out/fan-in dataflow: one rollout feeds parallel scoring / reference-logprob / value passes, which gather into an experience batch, which drives several PPO epochs, ending in a weight sync.

existing.name: openrlhf-ray-role-decomposition
existing.improvement: Reads clearly in both themes: the object store as a central hub with role actors around it is a strong, correct metaphor, and the orange sync_weights_to_vllm() loop back to the vLLM engine lands the key mechanism. No change needed beyond leaving it as the topology anchor for 6.5.2.

existing.name: nemo-aligner-megatron-3d-layout
existing.issue: The bottom TRT-LLM box is text-heavy: five stacked prose/italic lines plus two more small-print caption lines, which crowds the frame and, in dark mode, the 'weights copied... send/recv activations via NCCL' subtext under the NCCL-broadcast arrow washes out against the arrow. The 3D-parallelism grid on top (PP stages x TP shards x ghosted DP replicas) is the valuable part and is excellent.
existing.improvement: Trim the bottom box to at most three lines (compiled TRT engine; weights copied via NCCL broadcast after each update epoch; static SPMD topology fixed at init). Drop the two small-print caption lines entirely (the static-vs-dynamic point is now carried by the new orchestration-contrast figure). Move the arrow's 'send/recv activations via NCCL' note beside the arrow, not under it, so it does not collide in dark mode. Keep the top grid untouched.

new_figures.name: weight-sync-two-copies-bandwidth
new_figures.concept: Why the trainer must push weights into the rollout engine's read-only copy every update, and how the sync cost depends on the interconnect tier.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single most-repeated idea in the chapter (section 6.5.2, the memory example, section 6.5.7, the Interview Corner, and two Key Takeaways all hinge on it) yet no figure isolates it. The role-decomposition figure shows the sync arrow but not the crux: there are TWO live copies of the same policy, and keeping them coherent moves a huge tensor whose transfer time as a FRACTION of the update step is what forces the sync-frequency decision. A learner who groks this understands the whole flexibility-vs-throughput chapter.
new_figures.brief: A single static infographic in two vertically stacked bands. TOP BAND, 'Two copies of the policy': draw two model blocks side by side. Left block = 'Policy TRAINER' with a small gear/optimizer glyph and label 'trainable: fwd + bwd + Adam state'. Right block = 'Rollout ENGINE (vLLM)' labelled 'read-only copy, generation only'. A thick arrow flows LEFT to RIGHT labelled 'sync: load updated weights after each PPO update'. Show that the arrow carries a large tensor: annotate it '~140 GB bf16 for a 70B policy' (illustrative; keep the integer scheme as bf16=2 bytes, do not invent throughput). A thin dashed return arrow from the engine back to the trainer labelled 'rollout tokens + old logprobs' closes the loop so the reader sees the round trip. Under the sync arrow, a small caption: 'the engine's KV cache is discarded and generation restarts fresh'. BOTTOM BAND, 'The cost is bandwidth, and it competes with the update step': draw a horizontal bar-comparison of three interconnect tiers, ordered slowest-to-fastest or fastest-to-slowest, each a labelled bar whose LENGTH encodes relative transfer time for the SAME 140 GB payload: 'NVLink (intra-node)' = shortest bar, 'InfiniBand (multi-node)' = medium, 'PCIe' = longest. Do NOT print fabricated second-counts; instead label bars only with the ratio idea (e.g. 'fastest', 'mid', 'slowest') OR the interconnect name alone, and add one guiding sentence: 'sync overhead = transfer time / update-step time; large models amortize it, small models do not — hence sync every N steps.' Include a tiny inset showing the sync-frequency knob: two mini-timelines, 'sync every step' (frequent short sync marks) vs 'sync every N steps' (sparse), with the note 'less sync = staler rollout policy'. Theme-safe: use neutral fills for the two model blocks, a single accent for the sync arrow, grayscale bars with the accent only on the shortest/longest to cue the range. Everything must fit the viewBox with generous whitespace; no fabricated numbers beyond the illustrative 140 GB = 70e9 x 2 bytes derivation.

new_figures.name: ray-dynamic-vs-megatron-static-orchestration
new_figures.concept: The chapter's central thesis figure: dynamic task graph (Ray, OpenRLHF) vs static SPMD (Megatron, NeMo-Aligner) as two points in one design space.
new_figures.rationale: Section 6.5.5 ('The Fundamental Tension: Flexibility vs. Communication Efficiency') is the intellectual spine of the chapter and is currently served only by a comparison table. The distinction — a small Python controller dispatching futures to independently-sized, loosely-coupled actors whose every edge crosses a serialization boundary, versus one uniformly-sized SPMD grid pre-wired with NCCL and no controller in the hot path — is inherently spatial and clicks instantly as a side-by-side diagram. This figure is what lets a reader reason about WHY OpenRLHF is flexible-but-chatty and NeMo-Aligner is rigid-but-fast, which the whole decision guide (6.5.8) depends on.
new_figures.placement: The Fundamental Tension: Flexibility vs. Communication Efficiency (section 6.5.5)
new_figures.brief: Two side-by-side panels sharing one bottom trade-off axis. LEFT PANEL, 'Ray: dynamic task graph (OpenRLHF)': at the top a small box labelled 'Python controller (CPU driver)'. From it, dashed arrows labelled '.remote() future' fan out to FOUR role blocks drawn at deliberately DIFFERENT sizes to signal heterogeneous GPU counts: 'Policy (16 GPU, ZeRO-3)' large, 'Critic (4 GPU)' medium, 'Reference (8 GPU)' medium, 'Reward (2 GPU)' small. Every inter-block edge passes through a small labelled diamond 'serialize / object store' to make the per-edge cost visible. Caption strip: 'roles can differ in size and parallelism; controller coordinates; every crossing serializes.' RIGHT PANEL, 'Megatron: static SPMD (NeMo-Aligner)': NO controller box. Instead one uniform grid of identical worker cells (e.g. a 4x4 tile) all the same size, wired together with THICK solid lines labelled 'pre-established NCCL communicators (all-reduce / send-recv)'. A single side note: 'all processes launched together; comm plan fixed at init; no Python in the hot path.' Caption strip: 'every role must share the same parallelism config.' BOTTOM, spanning both panels: a horizontal double-headed axis with 'Flexibility (heterogeneous roles, rapid iteration)' anchored under the left panel and 'Communication efficiency (low latency, no serialization)' under the right, making explicit these are two ends of one trade. Optionally mark a small tick in the middle labelled 'veRL / HybridFlow: Ray orchestration + NCCL intra-group' to place the hybrid. Theme-safe: neutral fills; use one accent for the Ray dashed-future arrows and a DIFFERENT single accent (or thickness only) for the Megatron solid NCCL lines so the dashed-vs-solid, loose-vs-rigid contrast reads even in grayscale. Keep all labels legible, no overlaps, clear left-to-right reading order within each panel.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich and already has two solid figures: the OpenRLHF role/object-store topology (keep) and the Megatron 3D-parallelism layout (refine — trim its text-heavy bottom box). Two foundational intuitions are unserved: (1) the weight-sync duality and its bandwidth cost, the chapter's most-repeated engineering idea, and (2) the dynamic-task-graph (Ray) vs static-SPMD (Megatron) design-space thesis, currently carried only by a table. I propose one static figure for each. I deliberately do NOT propose an async-rollout-pipeline animation: the chapter explicitly defers that topic to the Prime-RL chapter (6.6), which already owns a sync-vs-async timeline figure family, so adding one here would duplicate.
